questions.prompt: Removing a public API function — semver bump?

choices.text: Major. Removing public API breaks consumers; the whole point of semver is communicating compatibility intent. Skipping major for 'just removing one thing' breaks consumers silently. When in doubt, bump major — overcautious annoys but protects.

questions.explanation: Removing public API = breaking change = major bump.

questions.prompt: Squash-and-merge team wants Conventional Commits in main history. How?

choices.text: Use PR titles as Conventional Commits. Squash-merge uses the PR title as the squash commit message. Add `amannn/action-semantic-pull-request` as a CI check that validates PR title format — PRs with non-conforming titles fail and can't merge. Main's history ends up clean and Conventional Commits-conformant. release-please can auto-generate changelogs from the squashed history. Document the format in PR template.

questions.explanation: PR titles + CI check validating title format. Squash uses title as commit message.

questions.prompt: Why has trunk-based replaced git-flow for most teams?

choices.text: Less merge pain (short branches diverge less), faster feedback (daily integration), simpler mental model (one branch to deploy), better fit for CI/CD. Git-flow's multiple long-lived branches accumulate divergence and ceremony. Vincent Driessen (git-flow's creator) himself wrote in 2020 that git-flow doesn't fit modern continuous-delivery teams. Git-flow still fits versioned products, regulated environments, or coordinated releases — minority cases now.

questions.explanation: Simpler model + faster feedback + better CI/CD fit. Trunk-based wins for most.

questions.prompt: Production broken from recent deploy. Default response?

choices.text: Rollback first, fix later. The previous deploy is built and known-good; promoting it (Vercel: one-click; tag-based: trigger rollback workflow) takes seconds and restores users to working state. Then diagnose calmly. Trying to debug under live-incident pressure produces hasty patches and second-incidents. Rollback is usually faster AND safer for the vast majority of incidents. Practice the rollback workflow monthly so the 3 AM use is muscle memory.

questions.explanation: Rollback first restores service immediately; diagnose calmly afterward.

questions.prompt: Why should DB migrations be backward-compatible?

choices.text: To enable code rollback without schema rollback. Code rollback is easy (move pointer to previous version); schema rollback is hard (might lose data; complex). If a migration adds a nullable column, old code still works after rollback (new column unused). If a migration drops a column, code rollback breaks because old code expects that column. Split breaking schema changes: Deploy 1 adds new column → Deploy 2 uses it → Deploy 3 drops old column. Between deploys, both code versions work; rollback within any window is safe. This pattern makes incident response dramatically simpler — rollback is always an option.

questions.explanation: Code rollback only works if schema doesn't drop/rename behind old code. Split breaking changes across deploys.
